subject: Company Law
module_title: Transactions with Directors and Connected Persons

questions.question: S238 CA 2014 restricts certain transactions between a company and its directors or connected persons involving non-cash assets. What is the 'requisite value' threshold that triggers the requirement for prior shareholder approval?

options.A: Any transaction with a director valued at more than €5,000
options.B: A transaction involving a non-cash asset worth more than €5,000 AND either more than €65,000 or more than 10% of the company's net asset value — whichever is the lesser of the two latter figures
options.C: A transaction involving a non-cash asset worth more than €65,000 only — the 10% net assets test is a separate alternative
options.D: Any non-cash transaction with a director regardless of value — S238 applies to all transactions without a minimum threshold

questions.explanation: S238 CA 2014 applies where the value of the non-cash asset: (i) exceeds €5,000; AND (ii) either exceeds €65,000 OR exceeds 10% of the company's net asset value. This is a conjunctive test — both limbs must be satisfied. The purpose is to exclude trivial transactions (below €5,000) while also capturing mid-range transactions relative to the company's size (the 10% net asset test).

questions.question: CarFlo Ltd, with net assets of €400,000, proposes to transfer a vehicle worth €45,000 to its managing director without shareholder approval. Does S238 CA 2014 apply?

options.A: No — €45,000 is less than the €65,000 fixed threshold, so S238 does not apply
options.B: Yes — €45,000 exceeds €5,000 AND represents more than 10% of net assets (10% of €400,000 = €40,000), so the requisite value test is met
options.C: No — S238 applies only to transactions with a value exceeding both €65,000 AND 10% of net assets simultaneously
options.D: Yes — but only if the vehicle was originally purchased using company funds within the last 12 months

questions.explanation: S238 triggers where the asset value exceeds €5,000 AND either: (a) exceeds €65,000, OR (b) exceeds 10% of net assets. Here, €45,000 > €5,000 and €45,000 > €40,000 (10% of €400,000). Both limbs are satisfied — the '10% of net assets' alternative applies even though €45,000 is under €65,000. Prior shareholder approval by ordinary resolution is therefore required.

questions.question: S238 CA 2014 requires 'prior approval by ordinary resolution' for qualifying transactions. Which of the following statements correctly describes the approval mechanism?

options.A: Approval must be by special resolution (75%) and filed with the CRO within 15 days
options.B: Approval must be by ordinary resolution (>50%) passed at a general meeting before the transaction is completed — a written resolution of shareholders may suffice if all members sign
options.C: Approval may be given retrospectively by the board of directors within 28 days of the transaction
options.D: Approval must be given by the audit committee of the company prior to the transaction

questions.explanation: S238 CA 2014 requires prior approval by the members of the company (ordinary resolution — simple majority). Under the Duomatic principle (Kerr v Conduit [2010] IEHC), informal unanimous shareholder consent is equivalent to a formal resolution, provided all members are fully informed and consent. Approval cannot be given by the board alone — the statute requires member approval to protect minority shareholders from insider transactions.

questions.question: UrbanSpaces Ltd has three shareholders who orally agree at an informal meeting that the company may transfer a warehouse worth €200,000 to its sole director, without a formal general meeting or written resolution. All three shareholders are present and agree. Under which principle might this arrangement be valid, and what is the leading Irish authority?

options.A: The arrangement is void — S238 requires a formal written resolution filed with the CRO
options.B: The Duomatic principle, applied in Kerr v Conduit Enterprises Ltd [2010] IEHC — unanimous informal assent by all members who are fully informed constitutes a valid resolution, even without a formal meeting or written instrument
options.C: The arrangement is valid under S161 CA 2014 — unanimous written resolutions are permitted for all acts
options.D: The arrangement requires no shareholder approval as the director is also the sole beneficial owner of all three shareholders

questions.explanation: The Duomatic principle (Re Duomatic Ltd [1969] 2 Ch 365, applied in Ireland in Kerr v Conduit [2010] IEHC and Buchanan Ltd v McVey [1954]) provides that unanimous informal assent by all members who are entitled to and capable of giving that assent constitutes a valid resolution, even without a formal general meeting or written resolution. Applied to S238 transactions, this means that where all members are fully informed and assent informally, the approval requirement can be satisfied — provided the assent is genuine and informed.

questions.question: A company enters into a substantial non-cash asset transaction with its director without obtaining prior shareholder approval in breach of S238 CA 2014. What is the legal consequence of this breach?

options.A: The transaction is void ab initio — it has no legal effect and cannot be affirmed
options.B: The transaction is voidable at the company's election — the company may either rescind it or affirm it; the director and any connected person must indemnify the company for any loss
options.C: The company is guilty of a category 2 criminal offence under S248 CA 2014
options.D: The director is automatically disqualified under S839 CA 2014 upon completion of an unauthorised S238 transaction

questions.explanation: S238(5) CA 2014 provides that a transaction in breach of S238 is voidable at the instance of the company — it is not automatically void. The company may elect to affirm (especially if the transaction is commercially beneficial) or rescind. In addition, the director who was party to the transaction and any person who procured it knowing of the breach is liable to account to the company for any gain made and to indemnify the company for any resulting loss.

questions.question: In Niltan Carson Ltd v Hawthorne [1988] ILRM, a meeting purportedly approving a substantial property transaction under the predecessor to S238 lacked a valid quorum. The High Court treated the approval as invalid. What does this case establish about the S238 approval requirement?

options.A: That a quorum defect at a shareholders meeting is always cured once a majority of members vote in favour
options.B: That the S238 approval must come from a properly constituted meeting — an approval voted on at a meeting lacking a valid quorum is not effective approval for S238 purposes
options.C: That only a written resolution can satisfy the S238 approval requirement, not a vote at a meeting
options.D: That S238 approval is not required where the director concerned waives the requirement

questions.explanation: Niltan Carson Ltd v Hawthorne [1988] ILRM 1 established that a purported shareholder approval at a meeting lacking a valid quorum does not constitute effective approval for S238 purposes. The statutory approval requirement is substantive, not merely formal — it must be obtained at a properly constituted meeting (or by valid equivalent consent). A defective meeting cannot supply the missing shareholder protection that S238 is designed to ensure.

questions.question: S220 CA 2014 defines 'connected person' for the purposes of S238 (and other provisions). Which of the following persons would be a connected person relative to Director Declan?

options.A: A senior employee of the company but not a director or shareholder
options.B: Declan's adult sister who is a shareholder in the company
options.C: A body corporate in which Declan is interested in shares conferring more than one-third of the voting power
options.D: Both B and C — the S220 definition encompasses family members and companies controlled by the director

questions.explanation: S220 CA 2014 defines connected persons broadly for the purposes of S238 and other provisions. Connected persons include: a spouse or civil partner; children (including stepchildren and adopted children); parents; siblings; a trustee of a trust under which the director or their family members are beneficiaries; a partner in a firm; and a body corporate in which the director holds shares carrying more than one-third of the voting power. Both Declan's sister (family member) and a company he controls (body corporate with significant voting interest) fall within S220.

questions.question: A company sells a property worth €1.2m to a director's spouse without obtaining shareholder approval under S238. Three years later the company enters liquidation. The liquidator discovers the transaction. Which of the following best describes the liquidator's options?

options.A: The liquidator has no remedy — the three-year delay means the company has affirmed the transaction
options.B: The liquidator may seek rescission of the transaction (if the property has not passed to a bona fide purchaser) and/or pursue the director and their spouse for an account of any gain and indemnity for any loss to the company — S238(5) CA 2014
options.C: The liquidator must establish fraudulent intent before any remedy is available
options.D: The liquidator may only recover the transaction under S604 CA 2014 (unfair preferences) — S238 cannot be invoked in liquidation

questions.explanation: S238(5) CA 2014 provides that on rescission of a voidable S238 transaction, the director and any connected person (here, the spouse) is liable to account for any gain and to indemnify the company for any loss. The liquidator steps into the shoes of the company and may pursue these remedies. The director's spouse is a connected person under S220. Note however that rescission may be barred if the property has passed to a bona fide third party purchaser without notice. The liquidator may also pursue the claim under S604 if it meets the preference criteria.

questions.question: EngineCo Ltd proposes to acquire machinery worth €80,000 from a company in which its sole director, Padraig, holds a 60% shareholding. The directors' net assets of EngineCo are €900,000. Does S238 apply and what is required?

options.A: S238 does not apply — the transaction involves an acquisition by the company, not a disposal to a director
options.B: S238 applies — the transaction is with a connected person (Padraig's 60% shareholding means he controls the counterparty company); €80,000 exceeds €5,000 and although it is less than €65,000 it does not exceed 10% of net assets (€90,000), so the 10% test is not met — check both limbs
options.C: S238 applies — €80,000 exceeds €5,000, and although it does not trigger the 10% net asset test (less than €90,000), it IS less than €65,000 — check both alternatives. Neither the €65,000 limb nor the 10% limb is met: S238 does NOT apply
options.D: S238 applies — any transaction with a company in which a director holds any shares is automatically within S238 regardless of value

questions.explanation: Working through the S238 requisite value test: the asset value (€80,000) exceeds €5,000 (limb 1 satisfied). For limb 2, the value must either exceed €65,000 OR exceed 10% of net assets. Here, €80,000 is greater than €65,000 (YES — limb 2 is satisfied). Therefore S238 DOES apply. The correct answer is that both limbs ARE met (€80,000 > €65,000), and the counterparty is a connected person (Padraig controls it with 60%). Prior shareholder approval is required. [Note: the answer choices are intentionally testing precise application of the two-limb test.]

questions.question: Which of the following transactions is expressly EXCLUDED from the S238 approval requirement under the exemptions in CA 2014?

options.A: A sale of company premises to the CEO's brother
options.B: A transaction between a company and its wholly-owned subsidiary where the subsidiary is the 'connected person'
options.C: A sale of company land to an unconnected third party at less than market value
options.D: An acquisition by the company of assets from a company in which the director holds 40% of voting shares

questions.explanation: S238 CA 2014 contains a number of exceptions from the approval requirement, including: (i) transactions between a company and its subsidiaries (group transactions); (ii) transactions in the ordinary course of business on ordinary commercial terms; and (iii) transactions with a value below the requisite value threshold. Wholly-owned group transactions are excluded because the economic reality is that the same ultimate owner controls both entities and there is no separate minority to protect in the subsidiary.
